title: Data Management and Sharing Plan
subtitle: From project systems to repository delivery
summary: An operational workflow entry showing how DMS planning becomes real work from source systems to structured data and repository delivery, with a live Builder-plus-review service that helps teams start, update, and review plans.
synopsis: The Data Management and Sharing Plan becomes useful when treated as an operational workflow rather than a compliance attachment. The deck follows the workflow implementation layer between local project systems and external repository delivery, including source data, raw structured data, derivatives, standards, validation, timing pressure, and the practical forms of support that matter in both planning and execution. It also presents a live Builder-plus-review service: the Builder helps teams work through sections and questions, generate markdown, and prepare an actual plan for expert review rather than pretending to automate the entire process.

slides.title: A DMS Plan Is A Workflow
slides.summary: A DMS Plan is a living workflow guide for what will actually happen to the data from collection through organization, transformation, documentation, and sharing.
bullets: A working guide for the data lifecycle
Useful only if it matches reality
slides.speech: A Data Management and Sharing Plan is not just a document that gets attached to a proposal and forgotten. It is a working guide for what will actually happen to the data over time. If it does not match the real lifecycle of the project, it stops being useful almost immediately.
slides.notes: Reframe the DMS Plan as operations, not paperwork.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[DMS Plan] --> B[Collection]
  B --> C[Organization]
  C --> D[Analysis and Derivatives]
  D --> E[Repository Delivery]

slides.title: Why This Matters Now
slides.summary: Many teams already know they need a DMS Plan and know where they are headed. The hard part is workflow implementation between project systems and repository delivery.
bullets: Teams know they need a DMS Plan
Teams often know the destination repository
The hard part is everything in between
That middle work needs structure and support
slides.speech: Many teams already know they need a Data Management and Sharing Plan. Many also know where they are ultimately headed, whether that is NDA or another repository. The hard part is everything in between. That workflow implementation work includes gathering files from multiple systems, deciding what counts as source data, cleaning and normalizing structure, building documentation and dictionaries, harmonizing variables, validating delivery packages, and preparing repository-facing outputs. This is the workflow implementation layer of research data work, and the plan only becomes meaningful when that layer is understood and supported.
slides.notes:
Use the essay logic here.
- teams know they need a plan
- teams often know the repository
- the hard part is workflow implementation
- support is needed between project systems and delivery
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Professional academic slide showing a researcher, project team, and data pathway to repository delivery, clean white background
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A clear pathway emerging from a dense field of inputs toward a defined destination

slides.title: Who Is Involved
slides.summary: The DMS environment includes the researcher, the project team, project systems, outside collaborators or entities, and the destination repository, all of which shape what the data must become.
bullets: Project systems such as REDCap and exports
slides.speech: The DMS environment is broader than one lab notebook or one storage folder. It includes the researcher, the principal investigator, the project team, project systems such as REDCap and exported tools, outside entities that hold or generate data, and the repository that will eventually receive the deliverable form of the dataset.
slides.notes: Help people see the full system instead of only local folders.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Researcher and PI] --> E[DMS Workflow]
  B[Project Team] --> E
  C[Project Systems] --> E
  D[Other Entities With Data] --> E
  E --> F[External Repository]

slides.title: Source Data
slides.summary: Source data is the original material gathered or received by a project, and it is often broad, messy, heterogeneous, and difficult to use directly for analysis or sharing.
bullets: NIH Toolbox or similar tool outputs
slides.speech: Source data is the original material collected across the project. It may come from REDCap, spreadsheets, CSV exports, NIH Toolbox files, technical systems, or outside collaborators. It often contains far more than what can be used directly, and it usually arrives in inconsistent shapes.
slides.notes: Let people recognize their real data chaos without embarrassment.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: A wall of varied research data sources including spreadsheets, REDCap exports, scanner files, and external files, organized but visibly heterogeneous
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Many differently shaped inputs flowing into one collection basin

slides.title: Raw Structured Data
slides.summary: Raw data is the cleaned, organized, and structured form prepared from source material so it can support analysis, documentation, and later sharing.
bullets: Structured for analysis and later delivery
slides.speech: Raw data is the organized version of source data. This is where naming becomes consistent, dictionaries become clearer, fields are normalized, and the dataset becomes usable for analysis rather than simply stored. Getting from source data to raw structured data is often one of the most labor-intensive parts of the workflow.
slides.notes: Emphasize that this transformation takes real judgment and labor.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Source Data] --> B[Cleaning]
  B --> C[Normalization]
  C --> D[Documentation]
  D --> E[Raw Structured Data]

slides.title: Derivatives
slides.summary: Derivatives are the outputs built from structured data, including analysis-facing products for research teams and repository-facing products for long-term access and reuse.
slides.speech: Derivatives are the outputs built from raw structured data. Some derivatives are for analysis, such as reports or models. Others are for sharing, such as harmonized files and submission-ready packages required by a repository. This is where projects often discover that they need more structure than they expected.
slides.notes: Differentiate internal research outputs from external sharing outputs.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: Research pipeline showing one cleaned dataset branching into analysis outputs and repository submission packages
slides.image_prompt_abstract: One ordered core splitting into two outward streams, one for insight and one for delivery

slides.title: Standards and Structures
slides.summary: Standards and formal structures make data understandable, reusable, interoperable, and deliverable by giving projects a consistent way to describe and package what they produce.
slides.speech: Standards matter because they make a dataset more understandable and more transferable. Depending on the project, this may mean BIDS, CSV plus data dictionary, repository-specific variables, or other structured layouts. The practical question is which structures matter for the project and how to use them coherently.
slides.notes: Make standards feel enabling and practical.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[Local Data] --> B[Standards]
  B --> C[BIDS]
  B --> D[CSV plus Dictionary]
  B --> E[Repository Structure]
  C --> F[Reusable Data]
  D --> F
  E --> F

slides.title: Tools and Workflows
slides.summary: The DMS Plan becomes real through concrete tools and workflows such as reports, data dictionaries, scripts, validation routines, transforms, and repeatable project procedures.
bullets: Bash and Python for repeated transformations
slides.speech: The DMS Plan becomes real through practical tools and workflows. REDCap, Excel, CSV files, Bash, Python, and careful documentation are often where the actual work happens. The work here is to shape those workflows coherently and carry them out in enough detail to support delivery.
slides.notes: Ground this in recognizable work products.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: A research operations workstation showing reports, spreadsheets, scripts, and documentation assembled into one repeatable workflow
slides.image_prompt_abstract: Rows of structured modules clicking into a process chain

slides.title: Repository Delivery
slides.summary: Repository delivery is where planning, documentation, data structure, standards, and technical labor finally converge into the package that must actually be submitted and understood by others.
bullets: Finish the job, not just discuss it
slides.speech: Repository delivery is where planning, standards, and technical labor finally converge. Repositories such as NDA require harmonization, validation, and recurring submissions. This is where many projects discover that they need help not just thinking about delivery, but actually completing it.
slides.notes: This is the operational climax of the deck.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Raw Structured Data] --> B[Harmonization]
  B --> C[Validation]
  C --> D[Submission Package]
  D --> E[Repository Delivery]
  E --> F[Recurring Updates]

slides.title: Timing and Pressure
slides.summary: DMS work is uneven and pressure-heavy because the hardest labor often concentrates around analysis milestones, manuscript preparation, repository submission, or grant deadlines.
bullets: Conditional rushes for analysis or sharing
slides.speech: Data management work is rarely evenly distributed across the life of a project. It often spikes around repository deadlines, annual reporting, or sudden needs for analysis and sharing. That is one reason DMS support matters. Projects need help when pressure is high, not only when planning is easy.
slides.notes: This is the operational argument for sustained execution support.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: A timeline with quiet periods interrupted by large peaks labeled repository deadline, annual review, and urgent analysis need
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A calm line interrupted by sharp peaks of effort and urgency

slides.title: Two Working Modes
slides.summary: This work helps in two practical ways: planning and workflow guidance for teams that need structure, and hands-on project help for teams that need substantial delivery work.
bullets: Planning: Builder, review, workflow design, repository guidance
Hands-on: restructuring, validation, harmonization, delivery work
Both tracks help the project move forward
slides.speech: There are two practical ways this work helps. In the planning mode, teams can use the Builder to work through the right sections and questions, generate markdown, and prepare a real plan for review. That review process helps clarify where the project is, what the DMS Plan should say, and what the workflow needs next. In the hands-on mode, the work shifts to organizing, validating, harmonizing, and preparing data for delivery.
slides.notes:
Present a clear service model.
- Builder plus review in the planning mode
- hands-on delivery work in the execution mode
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Project Need] --> B[Planning Mode]
  A --> C[Hands-on Mode]
  B --> D[Builder and Review]
  D --> E[Updated DMS Plan]
  C --> F[Deliverable Data]
  E --> G[Successful Project Progress]
  F --> G

slides.title: Core Message
slides.summary: The core message is that a DMS Plan is a living guide to real work, and this service helps make that work practical from early planning through repository delivery.
bullets: A DMS Plan is only useful if it matches reality
Projects need help with workflow implementation
Practical support can carry the work from structure through repository delivery
slides.speech: The core message is simple. A Data Management and Sharing Plan is only useful if it matches reality. The hardest work sits in workflow implementation between project systems and repository delivery. This service helps make that path practical, structured, and finishable.
slides.notes: End with calm confidence and operational clarity.
slides.image_prompt_concrete: A clean final slide showing the complete path from local project systems to successful repository delivery, polished academic style
slides.image_prompt_abstract: A complete pathway illuminated from start to finish
